fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that vector search utility we've been talking about. Basically developers should be able to throw a bunch of high-dimensional numeric arrays at it, search for the closest ones using a few different distance flavors, and get ranked results back. Think of it like what we did with the recommendation engine similarity layer, but standalone and reusable.

A few things I know we need: people should be able to tag their vectors with their own IDs instead of relying on internal slot numbers, search should work both in a fast approximate mode and a slow-but-exact mode, and results should survive a process restart — so some kind of save/load story is required.

We also had complaints from the data team about wasted space after they remove old entries — apparently once you delete something the slot just sits there. We need to let people reuse that capacity. And the index should be resizable if someone underestimates how many vectors they'll need upfront.

On the error side, if someone tries to pull vectors out before anything has been added, or passes in a single ID instead of a list, they should get a clean, human-readable error — not some Python stack trace or internal exception name leaking through.

Output format should be consistent and parseable — similar to the key=value style we standardized on in the metrics pipeline. Distances should be shown to 6 decimal places.

One more pass with a couple specifics the team asked for. The metric gets chosen when the index is constructed, and it has to be one of 'l2', 'ip', or 'cosine'. For 'l2' we mean squared Euclidean, so ranking is by the sum of squared coordinate differences. For 'ip' we mean inner product with distance = 1 - dot_product, so bigger dot products rank closer. For 'cosine' it’s cosine distance = 1 - cosine_similarity. One important gotcha on 'ip': because the reported distance is (1 - dot_product), vectors with large positive dot products can show up with negative distance values. That is expected. Example: querying [1,1,1] against itself gives distance -3.0, and that should be displayed as -3.000000.

Also, when I say approximate vs exact, the concrete classes here are the hnswlib Python bindings providing HierarchicalNSW (approximate) and BFIndex (brute-force exhaustive) index classes with add_items, knn_query, save_index, load_index, mark_deleted, unmark_deleted, resize_index, and get_items methods. For persistence, the HNSW side is saved with save_index(filepath) and loaded with load_index(filepath, max_elements=..., space=...). After loading, it still needs to accept more vectors through add_items. In the 'save_load_append' flow we save after first_batch_size vectors, then load and add the rest, and we expect 100% self-recall across all vectors. The loaded_then_count must equal the total vector count. BFIndex also supports save_index(filepath) and load_index(filepath), and after reload it should return identical labels and distances for the same query. On top of that, for 'replace_deleted_after_reload', it also needs to support Python pickle serialization via pickle.dumps / pickle.loads and still produce the same neighbor results after unpickling.

On the output contract, keep it very literal. Stdout is key=value lines, and each output line is a 'key=value\n' pair. For vector retrieval, the first line is either 'format=array' or 'format=list', then 'row_count=N', then 'items=[[v1,v2];[v3,v4]]'. Both 'array' and 'list' use the exact same items layout in stdout; the label just reflects what the caller asked for. Rows are semicolon-separated bracketed lists inside the outer brackets, and all float values are formatted to 6 decimal places. Same idea for neighbor results: label arrays look like [[a,b,c]] with no spaces, integer labels only, and distance arrays look like [[0.000000,1.000000]] with 6 decimal places. If there are multiple query rows in one batch, separate those rows with semicolons inside the outer brackets, like [[v1,v2];[v3,v4]].

Last thing, since self-recall came up: it is the fraction of queries where the query’s own label appears as the top-1 nearest neighbor. The calculation is (number of queries where result[0] == query_label) / total_query_count, formatted to 6 decimal places, so perfect recall is 1.000000. In both 'save_load_append' and 'resize_capacity', every stored vector is used as its own query, so perfect self-recall just means every vector retrieves itself.

trigger_keywords: what connectivity and construction width defaults should I use
what are the default HNSW graph parameters
what M and ef_construction values are expected
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default connectivity (M) is 16 and the default construction search width (ef_construction) is 100. The metadata output must report these as 'connectivity=16' and 'construction_width=100'. If a test case supplies a 'connectivity' field in input, that value overrides the default; construction_width always defaults to 100 unless otherwise specified.

trigger_keywords: what error is returned when no vectors have been added yet
how should empty store retrieval be handled
what happens if get_items is called on an empty index
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When retrieval is attempted before any vectors have been inserted, the output must be exactly 'error=items_unavailable\nstage=empty_store\n'. The error key must be 'items_unavailable' and the stage key must be 'empty_store'. No Python exception names, tracebacks, or runtime messages may appear in the output.

trigger_keywords: what error is raised when a scalar label is passed instead of a list
how should a single integer label input be handled for retrieval
what is the invalid label collection error format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When the caller passes a single scalar value where a collection of labels is required, the output must be exactly 'error=invalid_label_collection
label=<value>
' where <value> is the raw integer that was supplied. For example, if requested_label=5, output is 'error=invalid_label_collection
label=5
'. No exception class names may be exposed.

trigger_keywords: how does filtered search work
how do I restrict nearest-neighbor results to specific labels
what predicate format does the filter use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Filtered search accepts a Python callable (predicate function) that takes a label integer and returns True to allow it. The scenario 'filtered_even_search' uses a predicate that allows only even-numbered labels (label % 2 == 0). Both the approximate HNSW index and the brute-force index must support this predicate argument. Output reports approximate_labels, bruteforce_labels, the fraction of queries whose own label was selectable (exact_self_fraction), and odd_label_count which must be 0 when the even predicate is applied.

trigger_keywords: how does delete and restore work
can I undelete a vector after marking it deleted
what is the restore/undelete API
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The HNSW index supports marking labels as deleted via mark_deleted(label) which excludes them from search results. They can be restored via unmark_deleted(label) which makes them searchable again. The 'delete_restore' scenario deletes labels [1,3], verifies they don't appear in neighbors, then unmarks them and confirms 100% recall. The overlap count between deleted labels and post-delete neighbors must be 0.

trigger_keywords: how do I reuse slots after deletion
how does replace_deleted work for inserting into freed capacity
can I insert new vectors at full capacity after deleting old ones
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the HNSW index is initialized with allow_replace_deleted=True, new vectors can be added to an index at full capacity by occupying previously deleted slots. The add_items call should pass replace_deleted=True. After replacement, the index count stays at capacity, and the newly added vectors are findable by their new labels. This is demonstrated in 'replace_deleted' and 'replace_deleted_after_reload' scenarios.

trigger_keywords: how does index resizing work
can the capacity be increased after construction
what method resizes the index
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The HNSW index exposes resize_index(new_capacity) to increase maximum element capacity after construction. The 'resize_capacity' scenario creates an index with first_capacity=3, inserts 3 vectors, calls resize_index(6), then inserts 3 more vectors. Output must report capacity_before=3, capacity_after=6, count=6, all label IDs, and self_recall=1.000000.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning:
The error normalization contract: errors must output domain-specific keys like 'error=items_unavailable
stage=empty_store
' or 'error=invalid_label_collection
label=N
' and must never expose Python exception class names or runtime-generated messages in stdout

context_pointers.vague_reference: slots just sitting there after deletion
context_pointers.actual_meaning: HNSW index initialized with allow_replace_deleted=True, and add_items called with replace_deleted=True to fill previously mark_deleted slots without increasing capacity

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — could you check the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or look at how the hnswlib index methods are used in the scenario handlers? The test JSON files have exact expected outputs that should clarify the format.

feature1_1_metric_distance_ranking.json.description: A small vector collection is queried under each supported distance interpretation, and the nearest labels and distance values are reported in ranked order.

feature1_2_filtered_search.json.description: A nearest-neighbor query is executed with a predicate that permits only even-numbered labels, and both approximate and exhaustive search results must honor the predicate.

cases.expected_output:
metric=l2
query_count=10
approximate_labels=[0,0,2,4,4,4,6,8,8,8]
bruteforce_labels=[0,0,2,2,4,4,6,6,8,8]
approximate_exact_self_fraction=0.500000
bruteforce_exact_self_fraction=0.500000
approximate_odd_label_count=0
bruteforce_odd_label_count=0

feature2_1_retrieve_stored_vectors.json.description: Stored vectors are retrieved by their external labels, preserving requested order and supporting array-shaped and list-shaped output renderings.

feature2_2_retrieval_error_conditions.js.description: Retrieval requests that cannot identify a collection of stored labels are reported as normalized domain errors without exposing host-runtime exception names.

feature3_1_index_metadata.json.description: After vectors are inserted, the index reports its configured metric, dimensionality, graph construction parameters, capacity, and current element count.

cases.expected_output:
metric=l2
dim=2
connectivity=16
construction_width=100
capacity=6
count=3

feature4_1_save_load_and_append.json.description: An index saved after an initial batch can be loaded again, extended with another batch, and still return every stored vector as its own nearest neighbor.

feature4_2_bruteforce_save_load.json.description: An exhaustive vector store saved to disk and loaded again returns the same nearest labels and distances for the same query.

feature5_1_delete_and_restore_labels.jso.description: Deleted labels are excluded from nearest-neighbor results, and restoring those labels makes their vectors searchable again.

feature5_2_replace_deleted_slots.json.description: When replacement is enabled at fixed capacity, newly supplied vectors can occupy deleted slots and are retrievable by their new labels.

feature5_3_replace_deleted_after_reload..description: Deleted replacement slots remain reusable after file serialization and after object serialization, and the reloaded index finds the second replacement batch by its labels.

second_replacement_labels=[9,10,11]
loaded_neighbors=[9,10,11]
loaded_recall=1.000000
pickled_neighbors=[9,10,11]
pickled_recall=1.000000

feature6_1_resize_capacity.json.description: An index initialized with a smaller capacity can be resized upward, accept the remaining vectors, and report all labels while preserving self-neighbor search.
